Codeforces Round 1059 (Div. 3) Editorial
By wakanda-forever, history, 6 months ago,

2162A - Beautiful Average

Idea: wakanda-forever Preparation: wakanda-forever

Solution

Let's say we have a sequence of numbers with an average equal to kk.

Adding a number greater than kk increases the average, while adding a smaller one decreases it.

Therefore, the maximum average subarray is formed by taking the maximum element, since including anything smaller would only lower the average.

Hence, the final answer is the maximum element of the given array.

Time Complexity: O(n)O(n) per testcase.

Implementation
#include<bits/stdc++.h>
using namespace std;
 
#define ll long long
 
int main(){
 
    int t;
    cin >> t;
 
    while(t--){
        int n;
        cin >> n;
 
        vector<int> a(n);
        for(int i = 0; i < n; i++) cin >> a[i];
 
        cout << *max_element(a.begin(), a.end()) << endl;
    }
 
    return 0;
}

2162B - Beautiful String

Idea: wakanda-forever Preparation: tridipta2806

Solution

We need to find a non-decreasing subsequence pp such that, after removing its characters from the string ss, the remaining string xx becomes a palindrome.

Observe that if we choose pp to consist of all the 00's in ss, then xx will contain only 11's, which always forms a palindrome. Similarly, if we take pp as all the 11's, then xx will contain only 00's, which is also a palindrome.

In both cases, pp is clearly a non-decreasing subsequence (since all its elements are equal).

Therefore, a valid solution always exists — we can simply output the indices of either all 00's or all 11's in the string.

Time Complexity: O(n)O(n) per testcase.

Implementation
#include<bits/stdc++.h>
using namespace std;
 
#define ll long long
 
int main(){
 
    int t;
    cin >> t;
 
    while(t--){
        int n;
        cin >> n;
 
        string s;
        cin >> s;

        vector<int> pos;
        for(int i = 0; i < n; i++) if(s[i] == '0') pos.push_back(i + 1);

        cout << (int)pos.size() << '\n';
        for(auto& z : pos) cout << z << ' ';

        cout << '\n';
    }
 
    return 0;
}

2162C - Beautiful XOR

Idea: wakanda-forever Preparation: wakanda-forever

Solution

We are allowed to choose any xx such that 0≤x≤a0≤x≤a and set a:=a⊕xa:=a⊕x. Our goal is to make aa equal to bb.

Let's think of the binary representation of aa. Consider the highest set bit (most significant bit) of aa and let's assume its position is msb(a)msb⁡(a).

At each operation, we are choosing x≤ax≤a, which implies that msb(x)≤msb(a)msb⁡(x)≤msb⁡(a) (otherwise, we have x>ax>a).

a=(00…01……)2x=(00…0…1…)2a=(00…01……)2x=(00…0…1…)2

Now, we can surely say that msb(a⊕x)≤msb(a)msb⁡(a⊕x)≤msb⁡(a).

Thus, by performing the given operation, msb(a)msb⁡(a) will never increase. So, if msb(b)>msb(a)msb⁡(b)>msb⁡(a), the answer is −1−1.

Otherwise, we can always transform aa into bb in a constructive manner.

First, we set all bits below msb(a)msb⁡(a) one by one to ensure that every required bit can be manipulated. Then, for each bit that is 00 in bb, we unset it using an appropriate XOR operation.

By following this process, we can reach bb from aa in less than 6060 operations.

Time Complexity: O(log2(a))O(log2⁡(a)) per testcase.

Implementation
#include<bits/stdc++.h>
using namespace std;
 
int main(){
 
    int t;
    cin >> t;
 
    while(t--){
        int a, b;
        cin >> a >> b;
        
        if(__builtin_clz(a) > __builtin_clz(b)) cout << "-1\n";
        else if(a == b) cout << "0\n";
        else{
            vector<int> val;
            for(int i = 0; i < 31; i++){
                int x = (1 << i);
                if(x <= a && (a & x) == 0) a += x, val.push_back(x);
            }
            for(int i = 0; i < 31; i++){
                int x = (1 << i);
                if(x <= a && (b & x) == 0) val.push_back(x);
            }
            cout << val.size() << '\n';
            for(auto z : val) cout << z << ' ';
            cout << '\n';
        }
    }
}

2162D - Beautiful Permutation

Idea: wakanda-forever Preparation: wakanda-forever

Solution

We denote

query(1,l,r)=pl+pl+1⋯+prquery⁡(1,l,r)=pl+pl+1⋯+pr
query(2,l,r)=al+al+1⋯+arquery⁡(2,l,r)=al+al+1⋯+ar

Now, let us try to find ll first.

Consider two additional arrays representing the prefix sums of the arrays pp and aa, defined as follows:

pref_sum_p[i]=p1+p2+⋯+pi,1≤i≤npref_sum_p[i]=p1+p2+⋯+pi,1≤i≤n
pref_sum_a[i]=a1+a2+⋯+ai,1≤i≤npref_sum_a[i]=a1+a2+⋯+ai,1≤i≤n

Analysing these, we can say that pref_sum_a[i]≥pref_sum_p[i]pref_sum_a[i]≥pref_sum_p[i] for all ii. In fact, the inequality holds true only when l≤i≤nl≤i≤n.

Thus, we can binary search for ll.

At each stage, we make 22 queries query(1,1,mid)query⁡(1,1,mid) and query(2,1,mid)query⁡(2,1,mid), and by comparing these, we can adjust leftleft and rightright in binary search.

We can also find rr by querying suffixes in a similar way, but that would take a total of 4⋅⌈log2(n)⌉4⋅⌈log2⁡(n)⌉ queries. This exceeds the query limit.

Instead, we can query the whole array once and find rr. In other words,

query(2,1,n)−query(1,1,n)=r−l+1query⁡(2,1,n)−query⁡(1,1,n)=r−l+1

So, after finding ll, we can find rr in 22 queries. Total queries will be equal to 2⋅⌈log2(n)⌉+22⋅⌈log2⁡(n)⌉+2 (<40<40).

Time Complexity: O(log2(n))O(log2⁡(n)) per testcase.

Implementation
#include<bits/stdc++.h>
using namespace std;

#define ll long long

ll query(int type, int l, int r){
    ll x;
    cout << type << ' ' << l << ' ' << r << flush << endl;
    cin >> x;
    return x;
}
 
int main(){
 
    int t;
    cin >> t;
 
    while(t--){
        
        int n;
        cin >> n;

        ll sum = query(2, 1, n);
        sum -= ((n * (n + 1)) / 2);

        ll diff = sum - 1;

        int l = 1, r = n;

        while(l < r){
            int md = (l + r) / 2;
            ll val1 = query(1, 1, md);
            ll val2 = query(2, 1, md);

            if(val1 < val2) r = md;
            else l = md + 1;
        }        

        cout << "!" << ' ' << l << ' ' << diff + l << flush << endl;

    }
}

2162E - Beautiful Palindromes

Idea: wuhudsm Preparation: wakanda-forever

Solution

Instead of carefully choosing all kk elements individually, it is sufficient to pick three distinct integers xx, yy, and zz and repeat them cyclically — that is, append

x,y,z,x,y,z,…x,y,z,x,y,z,…

until we perform kk operations. For n≥3n≥3, we can always find such xx, yy, zz that add no extra palindromic subarrays.

Now let's try to find such integers xx, yy, zz. There will be two cases.

Case 1: aa is a permutation of 1,2,3,…,n1,2,3,…,n.

If the array aa is a permutation, we can simply choose the first three elements of aa for the construction. That is, let x=a1x=a1, y=a2y=a2, and z=a3z=a3. Since all elements in a permutation are distinct, these choices guarantee that xx, yy, and zz are distinct and add no extra palindromic subarrays.

Case 2: aa is not a permutation of 1,2,3,…,n1,2,3,…,n.

If the array aa is not a permutation, we can choose xx to be any integer that does not appear in aa. Let zz be the last element of the array, and choose yy as any integer different from both xx and zz. These three numbers will always be distinct and add no extra palindromic subarrays.

Time Complexity: O(n+k)O(n+k) per testcase.

Implementation
#include<bits/stdc++.h>
using namespace std;
 
int main(){
 
    int t;
    cin >> t;
 
    while(t--){
        
        int n, k;
        cin >> n >> k;

        vector<int> a(n), cnt(n + 1, 0);
        for(int i = 0; i < n; i++) cin >> a[i], cnt[a[i]]++;

        int x = -1;
        for(int i = 1; i <= n; i++) if(cnt[i] == 0){
            x = i;
            break;
        }

        if(x == -1){
            for(int i = 0; i < k; i++) cout << a[n - 3 + (i % 3)] << ' ';
            cout << '\n';
        }
        else{
            int y = -1;
            for(int i = 1; i <= n; i++) if(i != x && i != a[n - 1]){
                y = i;
                break;
            }
            vector<int> v = {x, y, a[n - 1]};
            for(int i = 0; i < k; i++) cout << v[i % 3] << ' ';
            cout << '\n';
        }

    }
}

2162F - Beautiful Intervals

Idea: frostcat, wuhudsm, wakanda-forever Preparation: wakanda-forever

Solution

Note that if [0,2,1][0,2,1] is a subsequence of permutation pp, any interval that includes 00 and 11 must also include 22. This implies that mexmex of any interval will not be equal to 22. So, 2∉M2∉M. Hence, mex(M)≤2mex⁡(M)≤2.

Thus, we can always ensure that mex(M)≤2mex⁡(M)≤2. Now, we only need to check when mex(M)=0mex⁡(M)=0 and mex(M)=1mex⁡(M)=1 are possible.

For mex(M)mex⁡(M) to be equal to 00, mexmex of every interval must be positive. So, every interval must include 00. This is possible only when all intervals intersect at a common point — that is, there exists at least one point that lies within every interval. Then we place 00 at some common point and fill the remaining permutation.

Otherwise, for mex(M)mex⁡(M) to be equal to 11, mexmex of each interval must not be equal to 11. To ensure this, we have to place 00 and 11 in such a way that if an interval includes 00, it must also include 11. This is possible if there exists a point that is not simultaneously the starting point of one interval and the ending point of another. In other words, we should be able to find a position that does not act as both a left and a right boundary. Then we place 00 at such a point and 11 adjacent to it.

Else, we place [0,2,1][0,2,1] as a subsequence first and then build the remaining permutation.

Time Complexity: O(n+m)O(n+m) per testcase.

Implementation
#include <bits/stdc++.h>
using namespace std;
#define int long long
#define INF (int)1e18

void Solve() 
{
    
    int n, m; cin >> n >> m;
    
    vector <int> l(m), r(m), f(n + 1), st(n + 1, 0), en(n + 1);
    for (int i = 0; i < m; i++){
        cin >> l[i] >> r[i];
        st[l[i]] = 1;
        en[r[i]] = 1;
        for (int j = l[i]; j <= r[i]; j++){
            f[j]++;
        }
    }
    
    vector <int> ans(n + 1, -1);
    
    auto fill = [&](){
        vector <bool> used(n, false);
        for (int i = 1; i <= n; i++) if (ans[i] != -1) used[ans[i]] = true;
        
        int p = 0;
        for (int i = 1; i <= n; i++){
            if (ans[i] == -1){
                while (used[p]) p++;
                
                ans[i] = p;
                used[p] = true;
            }
        }
        
        for (int i = 1; i <= n; i++){
            cout << ans[i] << " \n"[i == n];
        }
    };
    
    for (int i = 1; i <= n; i++){
        if (f[i] == m){
            int ptr = 1;
            ans[i] = 0;
            
            fill();
            return;
        }
    }
    
    for (int i = 1; i < n; i++){
        if (en[i] == 0){
            ans[i] = 0;
            ans[i + 1] = 1;
            fill();
            return;
        }
        
        if (st[i + 1] == 0){
            ans[i] = 1;
            ans[i + 1] = 0;
            fill();
            return;
        }
    }
    
    assert(n >= 3);
    ans[1] = 0;
    ans[2] = 2;
    ans[3] = 1;
    
    fill();
}

int32_t main() 
{
    ios_base::sync_with_stdio(0);
    cin.tie(0);
    int t = 1;
    cin >> t;
    for(int i = 1; i <= t; i++) 
    {
        Solve();
    }
    
    return 0;
}

2162G - Beautiful Tree

Idea: wakanda-forever Preparation: wakanda-forever

Solution

We have to construct a tree with nn vertices such that S=∑u,v∈E(u⋅v)S=∑u,v∈E(u⋅v) is a perfect square.

Instead of making it any random perfect square, let's try to make S=(f(n))2S=(f(n))2 where f(n)f(n) is an integer function of nn.

Now, let's check if we can make f(n)=nf(n)=n. For n=5n=5, we can consider the following tree

5−1−2−3−45−1−2−3−4

where S=25=(5)2S=25=(5)2.

Now that we know we have a tree TT with nn (=5=5) vertices having S=n2S=n2. Using this, let's try to make a tree T′T′ with n+1n+1 vertices such that S′=(n+1)2S′=(n+1)2. Note that S′−S=2n+1S′−S=2n+1. So, while constructing T′T′, we have to add a new vertex (n+1)(n+1) and also adjust this extra sum 2n+12n+1.

We can achieve this in the following manner:

  • add an edge between 11 and n+1n+1,
  • remove the edge between 11 and nn,
  • add an edge between 22 and nn.

This analogy can be extended for all n≥5n≥5. Such trees have the following structure:

So, we can always make S=n2S=n2 for all n≥5n≥5. For n<5n<5, we can just print sample outputs.

One can also assume f(n)=n+1f(n)=n+1 and construct a similar solution with S=(n+1)2S=(n+1)2.

Time Complexity: O(n)O(n) per testcase.

Implementation
#include<bits/stdc++.h>
using namespace std;
 
int main(){
 
    int t;
    cin >> t;
 
    while(t--){
 
        int n;
        cin >> n;
        
        if(n == 2){
            cout << -1 << '\n';
            continue;
        }
 
        if(n == 3){
            cout << "1 3\n2 3\n";
            continue;
        }
 
        if(n == 4){
            cout << "1 2\n3 1\n4 1\n";
            continue;
        }
 
        cout << "1 2\n";
        cout << "2 3\n";
        cout << "3 4\n";
 
        cout << "1 " << n << '\n';
 
        for(int i = 5; i < n; i++) cout << 2 << ' ' << i << '\n';
    }
}

2162H - Beautiful Problem

Idea: wuhudsm Preparation: wuhudsm

Spoiler

Solution

When the value of ff is 1 for [li,ri][li,ri], all numbers in [li,ri][li,ri] are either ≤x≤x or ≥x≥x.

We observe that for each interval, we need to divide the intervals into S-intervals (smaller) and B-intervals (bigger). S-intervals can only contain numbers ≤x≤x, and B-intervals can only contain numbers ≥x≥x.

Assuming we have fixed the S-intervals and B-intervals. For each index ii, there are 4 types:

  • Type 1: ii is covered only by S-intervals;
  • Type 2: ii is covered only by B-intervals;
  • Type 3: ii is covered by both S-intervals and B-intervals;
  • Type 4: ii is covered by neither S-intervals nor B-intervals.

Let cntscnts be the number of values <x<x, and cntbcntb be the number of values >x>x.

When there are no type 4 indices, we only need to check whether:

  • cnts≤cnts≤ (total number of type 1 indices)

  • cntb≤cntb≤ (total number of type 2 indices)

When type 4 indices exist, we only need to make a slight modification to the condition (this modification is left to the reader).

Consider a simpler case: all intervals are non-overlapping. In this case, the problem reduces to a classic knapsack problem, where each interval becomes an independent item. We consider extending the idea of DP to the original problem.

We find that intervals that are contained within others are useless and can be removed. Then, the intervals can be sorted by their left endpoints. This way, for i<ji<j, we can ensure li<ljli<lj and ri<rjri<rj. Then, we perform DP based on the order of the intervals.

We define:

dp[i][j][k=0 or 1]dp[i][j][k=0 or 1]: For the first ii intervals, the total number of type 1 indices is jj, and the last interval is an S-interval (k=0k=0) or a B-interval (k=1k=1), the maximum total number of type 2 indices obtained.

Let inslen[i]inslen[i] be the length of the intersection between the ii-th interval and the (i−1)(i−1)-th interval.

Transitions:

  • dp[i][j][0]=max(dp[i−1][j−inslen[i]][0],dp[i−1][j−inslen[i]][1]−inslen[i])dp[i][j][0]=max(dp[i−1][j−inslen[i]][0],dp[i−1][j−inslen[i]][1]−inslen[i])
  • dp[i][j][1]=max(dp[i−1][j][1]+inslen[i],dp[i−1][j+inslen[i]][1]+inslen[i])dp[i][j][1]=max(dp[i−1][j][1]+inslen[i],dp[i−1][j+inslen[i]][1]+inslen[i])

Note: For intervals (i−2)(i−2), (i−1)(i−1), ii, if the type of interval (i−1)(i−1) is different from both (i−2)(i−2) and ii, and r[i−2]≥l[i]r[i−2]≥l[i], some overlapping parts may be subtracted repeatedly. However, we find that this does not affect the final answer because it is not better than the answer when all three intervals are of the same type. Here, a common DP idea is applied: if "incorrect" states are recorded but they cannot be optimal, the DP transitions can remain unchanged (a similar idea is used in: https://codeforces.com/contest/1859/problem/E).

State 1 State 2
)
Some overlapping parts (in the middle) are subtracted repeatedly. Better because the value range of the middle positions is strictly larger.

Afterward, answering queries using the DP array is trivial. The time complexity is O(n2)O(n2).

There is another DP approach. Let b[i]b[i] be the smallest j≤ij≤i such that jj and ii are covered by the same interval. Then, if position ii is filled with a number <x<x, we can deduce that all numbers in a′[b[i],…,i]a′[b[i],…,i] must be <x<x, and so on. This way, we can write a DP that transitions position by position, without pre-processing each interval.

For details, refer to Dominater069 's code.

Implementation1(wuhudsm)
#include <map>
#include <set>
#include <cmath>
#include <ctime>
#include <queue>
#include <stack>
#include <cstdio>
#include <cstdlib>
#include <vector>
#include <cstring>
#include <algorithm>
#include <iostream>
#include <bitset>
#include <unordered_map>
#define fastio ios_base::sync_with_stdio(false);cin.tie(0);cout.tie(0);
using namespace std;
typedef double db;
typedef long long ll;
typedef unsigned long long ull;
const int N=3010;
const int LOGN=28;
const ll  TMD=0;
const ll  INF=100000000;
int T,n,m;
int a[N],cnt[N],mxr[N];
int dp[N][N][2];

/*
 * stuff you should look for
 * [Before Submission]
 * array bounds, initialization, int overflow, special cases (like n=1), typo
 * [Still WA]
 * check typo carefully
 * casework mistake
 * special bug
 * stress test
 */

//--------------------------------------------------------------------------

struct seg
{
	int l,r;

	seg() {}

	seg(int l,int r):l(l),r(r){}

	int len()
	{
	    return r-l+1;
	}
};

seg getins(seg x,seg y)
{
	if(x.l>y.l) swap(x,y);
	if(x.r<y.l) return seg(INF,INF);
	if(x.r<y.r) return seg(y.l,x.r);
	else return seg(y.l,y.r);
}

//--------------------------------------------------------------------------

void init()
{
    cin>>n>>m;
    for(int i=0;i<=n;i++)
        mxr[i]=0;
    for(int i=1;i<=n;i++)
        cin>>a[i];
    for(int i=1;i<=m;i++)
    {
        int l,r;
        cin>>l>>r;
        mxr[l]=max(mxr[l],r);
    }
    for(int i=0;i<=n+4;i++)
        for(int j=0;j<=n+4;j++)
            dp[i][j][0]=dp[i][j][1]=-INF;
}

void solve()
{
    int sz,cfree=0;
    vector<int> tag(n+4);
    vector<seg> vs;
    for(int i=1;i<=n;i++)
    {
        if(!mxr[i]) continue;
        if(vs.empty()||vs.back().r<mxr[i])
            vs.push_back(seg(i,mxr[i]));
    }
    sz=vs.size();
    dp[1][0][0]=vs[0].len();
    dp[1][vs[0].len()][1]=0;
    for(int i=2;i<=sz;i++)
    {
        seg cur=vs[i-1],ins=getins(vs[i-2],vs[i-1]);
        int len_ins=(ins.l==INF?0:ins.len());
        for(int j=0;j<=n;j++)
        {
            dp[i][j][0]=max(dp[i][j][0],dp[i-1][j][0]+cur.len()-len_ins);
            if(j+len_ins<=n)
                dp[i][j][0]=max(dp[i][j][0],dp[i-1][j+len_ins][1]+cur.len()-len_ins);
            if(j-(cur.len()-len_ins)>=0)
            {
                dp[i][j][1]=max(dp[i][j][1],dp[i-1][j-(cur.len()-len_ins)][1]);
                dp[i][j][1]=max(dp[i][j][1],dp[i-1][j-(cur.len()-len_ins)][0]-len_ins);
            }
        }
    }
    for(int i=0;i<vs.size();i++)
        for(int j=vs[i].l;j<=vs[i].r;j++)
            tag[j]=1;
    for(int i=1;i<=n;i++)
        if(!tag[i]) cfree++;
    for(int i=1;i<=n;i++)
    {
        int s=0,b=0;
        for(int j=1;j<=n;j++)
        {
            if(a[j]<i) s++;
            else if(a[j]>i) b++;
        }
        int flg=0;
        for(int j=0;j<=n;j++)
        {
            int need1=max(0,s-j),need2=max(0,b-max(dp[sz][j][0],dp[sz][j][1]));
            flg|=(cfree>=need1+need2);
        }
        cout<<flg;
    }
    cout<<"\n";
}

//-------------------------------------------------------

void gen_data()
{
    srand(time(NULL));
}

int bruteforce()
{
    return 0;
}

//-------------------------------------------------------

int main()
{
    fastio;

    cin>>T;
	while(T--)
	{
		init();
		solve();

		/*

		//Stress Test

		gen_data();
		auto ans1=solve(),ans2=bruteforce();
		if(ans1==ans2) printf("OK!\n");
		else
		{
			//Output Data
 		}

		*/
	}

	return 0;
}
Implementation2 (Dominater069)
#include <bits/stdc++.h>
using namespace std;
#define INF (int)1e9

mt19937_64 RNG(chrono::steady_clock::now().time_since_epoch().count());

void Solve() 
{
    // x <= min or x >= max 
    // min < x < max is bad 
    // => in range if both smaller and larger exists, cooked 
    // just spam smaller at one end, and larger at other end? 
    // i <-> j if there exists interval covering both 
    // if i <-> j, cannot have diff type on i and j 
    // dp[i][number of 0s] = max number of 1s, but also need to know what is the last 0 / 1 position 
    // we can extend last character always 
    // we can extend other character only after some gap 
    // prefmax?
    
    int n, m; cin >> n >> m;
    
    vector <int> a(n + 1), f(n + 1);
    for (int i = 1; i <= n; i++){
        cin >> a[i];
        f[a[i]]++;
    }
    
    vector <int> b(n + 1), c(n + 1);
    for (int i = 1; i <= n; i++){
        b[i] = c[i] = i;
    }
    for (int i = 1; i <= m; i++){
        int l, r; cin >> l >> r;
        for (int j = l; j <= r; j++){
            b[j] = min(b[j], l);
            c[j] = max(c[j], r);
        }
    }
    
    // vector dp(n + 1, vector(n + 1, vector <int>(2, -INF)));
    // vector pref(n + 1, vector(n + 1, vector <int>(2, -INF)));
    int dp[n + 1][n + 1][2];
    int pref[n + 1][n + 1][2];
    
    for (int i = 0; i <= n; i++){
        for (int j = 0; j <= n; j++){
            for (int k = 0; k < 2; k++){
                dp[i][j][k] = -INF;
                pref[i][j][k] = -INF;
            }
        }
    }
    
    dp[0][0][0] = dp[0][0][1] = 0;
    pref[0][0][0] = pref[0][0][1] = 0;
    
    for (int i = 1; i <= n; i++){
        for (int x = 0; x <= n; x++){
            for (int y = 0; y < 2; y++){
                for (int z = 0; z < 2; z++){
                    if (y == z){
                        int px = x - (y == 0);
                        if (px >= 0){
                            dp[i][x][y] = max(dp[i][x][y], pref[i - 1][px][z] + (y == 1));
                        }
                    } else {
                        int px = x - (y == 0);
                        int lim = b[i] - 1;
                        if (px >= 0){
                            dp[i][x][y] = max(dp[i][x][y], pref[lim][px][z] + (y == 1));
                        }
                    }
                }
                
                pref[i][x][y] = max(pref[i - 1][x][y], dp[i][x][y]);
            }
        }
    }
    
    for (int i = 1; i <= n; i++){
        int lower = 0;
        int upper = 0;
        for (int j = 1; j < i; j++) lower += f[j];
        for (int j = i + 1; j <= n; j++) upper += f[j];
        
        if (pref[n][lower][0] >= upper || pref[n][lower][1] >= upper){
            cout << "1";
        } else {
            cout << "0";
        }
    }
    cout << "\n";
}

int32_t main() 
{
    auto begin = std::chrono::high_resolution_clock::now();
    ios_base::sync_with_stdio(0);
    cin.tie(0);
    int t = 1;
    // freopen("in",  "r", stdin);
    // freopen("out", "w", stdout);
    
    cin >> t;
    for(int i = 1; i <= t; i++) 
    {
        //cout << "Case #" << i << ": ";
        Solve();
    }
    auto end = std::chrono::high_resolution_clock::now();
    auto elapsed = std::chrono::duration_cast<std::chrono::nanoseconds>(end - begin);
    cerr << "Time measured: " << elapsed.count() * 1e-9 << " seconds.\n"; 
    return 0;
}

    ksandr1v
    5 months ago, show #

    ksandr1v
    5 months ago, hide # ^ |
    ← Rev. 3 →  
    0

    If ai≠ai−1 && ai≠ai−2ai≠ai−1 && ai≠ai−2 for all i, then the array is guaranteed to not contains palindromes of length > 1. Proof: palindromes 2 and 3 do not exist by condition. Others palindromes contain palindromes 2 or 3.

    → Reply

    marcus06
    5 months ago, show #

    »
    marcus06
    5 months ago, hide # ^ |
    ← Rev. 2 →  
    0

    my intuition for E was trying not to add number that can create palindrome of length 2 or length 3 (when we append a[x], try to make a[x] != a[x — 1] and a[x] != a[x — 2]). The code was:

    int v1 = a[x — 1], v2 = a[x — 2];

    int v3 = v1;

    while (v3 == v1 || v3 == v2) v3 = v3 % n + 1;

    But then i realized that my solution failed to this testcase : 4 1 4 3 2 3 (my code will output 4, which will make a palindrome of length 5). So to avoid this i will try to add number that hasn't appear in original array, then follow the above algorithm.

    → Reply

5 months ago, hide # |
← Rev. 2 →  
+3
Simple solution to G

The sample has constructions for n≤4n≤4, so assume n>4n>4. Start by constructing a star where all nodes are connected to node 11. This tree has a value of S=2+3+...+nS=2+3+...+n. Let xx be the smallest number such that x2≥Sx2≥S and d=x2−Sd=x2−S. If d>2d>2, we can detach some nodes from 11 and connect them to a greater node to increase the tree's value to x2x2. For x=1,2x=1,2, we can instead form a sum of (x+1)2(x+1)2.

→ Reply

    Andimeo
    5 months ago, show (+3) #
    »
    »
    Andimeo
    5 months ago, hide # ^ |
     
    +3

    Seems we can't construct an answer for n=5n=5 with this method.

    S=2+3+4+5=14S=2+3+4+5=14. d=16−14=2d=16−14=2.

    I assume you mean d>2d>2 by "If x>2x>2". Since d=2d=2, we fall back to d=25−14=11d=25−14=11. But 3,4,53,4,5 can't add up to 11.

  • »
    »
    dominique38
    5 months ago, show (+2) #
    »
    »
    dominique38
    5 months ago, hide # ^ |
    ← Rev. 4 →  
    +7

    It's a good solution indeed. Hopefully not just for myself, the crucial part feels a little vague.

    we can detach some nodes from 11 and connect them to a greater node to increase the tree's value to x2x2, we can instead from a sum (x+1)2(x+1)2.

    My attempt to explain it more elaborately.

    1. Claim 1: xx being the smallest integer such that x2≥Sx2≥S . Then for all n≥5n≥5 , we have n>xn>x and ⌈(n+1)/2–√⌉≥x⌈(n+1)/2⌉≥x .
    Proof

    S=2+3+4+⋯+n=n(n+1)2−1S=2+3+4+⋯+n=n(n+1)2−1 .

    The first inequality n>xn>x can be proven via proof by contradiction and, showing it's only possible for n<4n<4. Lets say x=nx=n, which means x2≥Sx2≥S and (x−1)2<S(x−1)2<S.

    (n−1)⋅(n−1)<n(n+1)2−1n⋅(n−1)−(n−1)<n(n+1)2−1n⋅(n−1)2+n⋅(n−1)2−(n−1)<n(n+1)2−1(n−1)⋅(n−1)<n(n+1)2−1n⋅(n−1)−(n−1)<n(n+1)2−1n⋅(n−1)2+n⋅(n−1)2−(n−1)<n(n+1)2−1

    I can break n(n−1)n(n−1) into parts since it's an even integer; I need to be careful when dealing with integers.

    n⋅(n−1)2−(n−1)<n(n+1)2−n⋅(n−1)2−1n⋅(n−1)2−(n−1)<n−1n⋅(n−1)2<2(n−1)n⋅(n−1)<4(n−1)n<4n⋅(n−1)2−(n−1)<n(n+1)2−n⋅(n−1)2−1n⋅(n−1)2−(n−1)<n−1n⋅(n−1)2<2(n−1)n⋅(n−1)<4(n−1)n<4

    Since n>1n>1, we can cancel on both sides, which yields the result that nn must be less than 44 and leads to a contradiction. The upper bound is quite loose. For the trivial case where x2=Sx2=S (as with n=4n=4), we've already found our answer. The only case remaining is n≥5n≥5, and we now just need to prove that x2−S>0x2−S>0.

    We will use two properties of the ceiling function:
    1. If x1≤x2x1≤x2, then ⌈x1⌉≤⌈x2⌉⌈x1⌉≤⌈x2⌉.
    2. ⌈x1⋅x2⌉≤⌈x1⌉⋅⌈x2⌉⌈x1⋅x2⌉≤⌈x1⌉⋅⌈x2⌉.

    (⌈(n+1)2–√⌉⋅⌈(n+1)2–√⌉)−n(n+1)2+1⌈(n+1)22⌉−n(n+1)2+1⌈(n+1)22⌉−⌈n(n+1)2⌉+1≥x2−S≥x2−S≥x2−S(⌈(n+1)2⌉⋅⌈(n+1)2⌉)−n(n+1)2+1≥x2−S⌈(n+1)22⌉−n(n+1)2+1≥x2−S⌈(n+1)22⌉−⌈n(n+1)2⌉+1≥x2−S

    Using the first ceiling property, we can say that the first ceiling term is at least as big as the second term. Therefore, we can see that the LHS is at least 11 integer larger.

    It feels like I have overcomplicated things very much. Sorry.

    Corollary: Taking d=x2−Sd=x2−S, then 2S−−√>d2S>d must be true.

    Proof

    If x2≥Sx2≥S, then (x−1)2<S(x−1)2<S. This implies that x≤⌈S−−√⌉+1x≤⌈S⌉+1.

    ddd=x2−S≤(⌈S−−√⌉+1)2−S≤2⌈S−−√⌉+1+(⌈S−−√⌉)2−Sd=x2−Sd≤(⌈S⌉+1)2−Sd≤2⌈S⌉+1+(⌈S⌉)2−S

    From the Ceiling property, we can say the remaining terms are greater than 0. Therefore, d<2⌈S−−√⌉d<2⌈S⌉. This is approximately d≈2–√nd≈2n. In practice, its d/nd/n ratio is ≈1.45455≈1.45455.

    Now, our initial tree is rooted at 11, and all other nodes are directly connected to 11 as leaves.  Now I want to rearrange the leaves such that my sum is increased by d(=x2−S)d(=x2−S). 

    Let's say we pluck the leaf vv and attach it to uu. Then our new sum, SnewSnew, is calculated as:

    Snew=S−v+uv=S+v(u−1)Snew=S−v+uv=S+v(u−1)

    If the required increment satisfies $d \leq n$, we can choose the parent u=2u=2 and the leaf v=dv=d. Our job will be done in a single step. If d>nd>n, we need a multi-step approach. We claim that we can always find v1v1 and v2v2 such that n≥v1,v2>2n≥v1,v2>2 and their sum is dd.

    One possible method for partitioning dd into two parts (given d>nd>n):

    1. If dd is odd: Choose v1=⌊d/2⌋v1=⌊d/2⌋ and v2=⌊d/2⌋+1v2=⌊d/2⌋+1.

    2. If dd is even: Choose v1=d/2−1v1=d/2−1 and v2=d/2+1v2=d/2+1.

    For n≥5n≥5, these vertices v1v1 and v2v2 will be ≥3≥3 in both cases.

    But if d=1d=1 or d=2d=2, we need to be a little careful.

    Case 1: If d=2d=2, then 2=x2−S2=x2−S. We can rewrite this as 2+2x+1=(x+1)2−S2+2x+1=(x+1)2−S. Let's break the LHS into 2 parts, 3+2x3+2x. We need to achieve a 2x2x and a 33 increment. We can get that easily in 2 steps: plucking xx and attaching it to 33 (which will give a 2x2x increment), and then joining 33 to 22 (which will give a 33 increment).

    Case 2: If d=1d=1, then 1+2x+1=(x+1)2−S1+2x+1=(x+1)2−S. Let's make the LHS into 2(x+1)2(x+1). Then we can always pluck x+1x+1 and attach it to 33, giving us a 2x+22x+2 increment. This is a legal move since x+1≤nx+1≤n . 

    Submission link 344535551

    Edit1:

    Mistake: In calculating x2x2 and in using the Ceiling property ⌈x1⋅x2⌉≤⌈x1⌉⋅⌈x2⌉⌈x1⋅x2⌉≤⌈x1⌉⋅⌈x2⌉, I mistakenly took the lower bound of this product when determining the upper bound. That was a stupid mistake.

    The following edits are made:

    1. In the Proof section, it is acceptable to take the lower bound when proving that xx indeed follows this inequality: ⌈(n+1)/2–√⌉≥x⌈(n+1)/2⌉≥x.

    2. In the Corollary Proof, a much easier and tighter bound to prove is 2S−−√>d2S>d.

    3. Handling the case when d>2d>2: Since dd is the required increment, we can always find v1v1 and v2v2 such that n≥v1,v2>2n≥v1,v2>2 and their sum is dd.

    → Reply

    I'm getting wrong output / not working properly. Can someone please help me find the issue in my code?

    Here’s my code:

    #include <bits/stdc++.h>
using namespace std;

void solve() {
    long long n, k;
    cin >> n >> k;
    vector<int> v2(n);
    for (int i = 0; i < n; i++) cin >> v2[i];
    vector<int> v(3);
    map<int, int> mpp;
    int j = 2;
    for (int i = n - 1; i >= 0; i--) {
        if (mpp.find(v2[i]) == mpp.end()) {
            mpp[v2[i]]++;
            v[j--] = v2[i];
        }
        if (j == -1) break;
    }
    if (j >= 0) {
        for (int i = 1; i <= 3; i++) {
            if (mpp.find(i) == mpp.end()) {
                v[j--] = i;
                mpp[i]++;
            }
            if (j == -1) break;
        }
    }
    for (int i = 0; i < k; i++) cout << v[i % 3] << " ";
    cout << '\n';
}

int main() {
    ios::sync_with_stdio(false);
    cin.tie(nullptr);
    int t;
    cin >> t;
    while (t--) solve();
}

    Please tell me what’s wrong or how to fix it. Thanks!

+1

my solution for problem c

#include <bits/stdc++.h>
using namespace std;

int main(){
    long long t;
    cin >> t;
    while(t--){
        int n,m;;
        cin >> n >> m;
        int x = 1;
        while(n >= x){
            x = x << 1;
        }
        x--;
        int x1 = n^x;
        int x2 = m^x;
        if(m > x){
            cout << -1 << '\n';
        }else{
            cout << 2 << '\n';
            cout << x1 << ' ' << x2 << '\n';
        }

    }
}

    could you please explain for me it from math perspectives?

    • »

      taagcgaat
      5 months ago, show (+1) #

      »
      taagcgaat

      0

      why not use only n^m?

      → Reply
      • »
        »
        »
        »
        mayank8127
        5 months ago, show #
        »
        »
        »
        »
        mayank8127
        5 months ago, hide # ^ |
         
        0

        we cant directly use n^m because for cases where the MSB of m is less than n, n^m can be greater than n which will violate the codition

        Example n = 1000, m = 0001

        then xor will be 1001

        for this reason we can do it by first taking it to the largest possible value that is 1111 then going to 0001'

        in this way we will always be able to do it in 2 steps

        → Reply
    • »
      »
      »
      mayank8127
      5 months ago, show #
      »
      »
      »
      mayank8127
      5 months ago, hide # ^ |
       
      0

      See in this problem as they have explained in the editorial it is not possible to convert a to b if b has a bigger MSB than a, otherwise it is always solvable.

      What is being done in this solution is that first we are finding the biggest number x that is possible which will be having all ones till the MSB of a. To find that we are first just finding the first power of two which is bigger than a and then when we subtract one from it we will turn the only bit of that power of two to zero and all other smaller bits to one.

      For example:

      Lets say a is 1010

      And b is 0100

      Now as b is smaller than 1111 it is clearly reachable

      So with the while loop we get 10000 which is the first square bigger than 1010

      Then we subtract one getting 1111.

      We did all this because we want to find a number x1 which on doing xor with 1010 will give us 1111 To find that we can now just simply xor 1010 with 1111 which will give us 0101 as the first number that we want to print.

      It is true because for three numbers x,y,z if x xor y equals z then you can interchange there positions in any way and it will always be true.

      So instead of doing a xor x1 to get 1111

      We did a xor 1111 to get x1

      Now from 1111 we have to get to 0100

      So essentially 1111 xor x2 equals 0100

      This can similarly also be done as 1111 xor 0100 to get x2.

      So x2 will be 1011

      And in this way x1 and x2 will be our solution.

      Hope this helped.

      → Reply

Soultaker
5 months ago, show (+3) #
»
Soultaker
5 months ago, hide # |
 
+1

For 2162G - Beautiful Tree I had a different construction, which works for n>5n>5. For smaller nn you can just brute force solutions by trying all trees, or hardcoding the solutions (the first four cases are already given in the sample input).

Spoiler

The minimum value tree has all vertices v>2v>2 connected to vertex 1, with a sum s=2+3+4+..+n=n(n+1)/2−1s=2+3+4+..+n=n(n+1)/2−1

When ss happens to be square, you are done. Otherwise, let s′s′ be the smallest square greater than ss. We need to increase the value of the tree by x=s′−sx=s′−s. It can be shown that x<2nx<2n.

To increase the value of the tree, we can take a vertex vv (2<v≤n2<v≤n) and change its parent from 1 to 2 to increase the sum by exactly vv.

So when 2<x≤n2<x≤n we can simply reparent vertex xx. If n<x<2nn<x<2n we need to reparent a pair of elements that sum to xx, subject to the constraints the they must be different and both are between 33 and nn. A simple solution is to pick vertex x/2−1x/2−1 and x/2+1x/2+1 if xx is even, or (x−1)/2(x−1)/2 and (x+1)/2(x+1)/2 if xx is odd.

The only tricky case is when 0<x≤20<x≤2 because the vertices to be moved must be greater than 2. In that case, we simply use the next square after s′s′ instead; it can be shown that this is still less than 2n2n away so we still only need to move 2 vertices.

→ Reply

    For n=5, s is 14, s' is 16 so x=2, which falls in your last case, so for next s' you will try s'=25, but here x becomes 25-14=11, which is greater than 2*n (10), so that contradicts your explanation for the last case right? Although we can still make 25 using some arrangement.

+5

Randomised solution for G:

Separate 1 from other vertices. Generate a random tree on vertices (I didn't even do some uniform distribution of trees, just generate the parent and then relabel the vertices randomly), and then try to connect 1 to some vertex.

    Very nice solution!

    To clarify, like implementation-wise, does this mean that you permute 2..n randomly, attach them into a star, then try to attach 1 somewhere?

    Also did you prove it or was it intuitively obvious that it would work within TL?

      Noobish_Monk
      5 months ago, hide # ^ |
       
      0

      I didn't attach 2..n in a star. I created a random tree where parentv<vparentv<v and then relabeled the vertices. As for why it should work within TL I don't really have a proof (and my first implementation barely passed), but the amount we need to add for a number to be a perfect square is up to 2n2n and we can add up to nn, so it seems like we only need a small number of checks

      → Reply
  • »
    »
    d1amondzz
    5 months ago, show #
    »
    »
    d1amondzz
    5 months ago, hide # ^ |
     
    +10

    Your solution actually gave me the intuition of how to get a deterministic solution, so thank you for that.

    We try to form some tree without 1 and add something from 2 to n using the edge with 1 to get to a perfect square.

    If we use a star tree with 2 in the center we get the sum 2*(3+n)(n-2)/2=n+n^2-6. It's greater than n^2 in most cases, so let's try (n+1)^2:

    (n+1)^2-n-n^2+6=n+7 > n, so let's try to increase the sum of the star tree by something >= 7, for example by connecting 5 to 4 instead of 2.

    Then the sum will increase by 5*4-5*2=10, so we'll need to add n+7-10=n-3. So the construction for n>=5 is:

    1 n-3

    2 3

    2 4

    2 i, 6 <= i <= n

    4 5

    → Reply

In the fourth test case, we remove p=010 (indices 3, 4, 6), resulting in x=110011, which is a palindrome. which should be---- In the fourth test case, we remove p=01 (indices 3, 4), resulting in x=110011, which is a palindrome.

baldy

0 
#include <bits/stdc++.h>
using namespace std;
#define int long long
void solve(){
  int n,k; cin >> n >> k;
  vector<int> v(n);
  for(int i =0;i < n;i++) cin >> v[i];
  int ans = *max_element(v.begin(),v.end()) + 1;
  for(int i =0;i < k;i++){
	  cout << ans << " ";
	  ans++;
  }
  cout << endl;
}
signed main(){
	int tt = 1; cin >> tt;
	while(tt--){
		solve();
	}
	return 0;
}

Will this code for E pass, as the system testing is going on i cannot check this and i didn't want to see the editorial section.

Logic: So the question has asked us to minimize the number of palindromic subarray. So to do that we can just append the maximum element in the array + 1 to the end and then increase this variable by 1 every time so that it will not form any palindromic subarray with the previous ones.

An easier sol'n to C would be, if highest set bit of B exceeds A, cout -1. else set all the bit of A to 1 using its compliment then converting A to B by A xor compliment of B.

    C has another neat solution. If a⊕b≤aa⊕b≤a, we just print a⊕ba⊕b. Else if b≤ab≤a, print bb then aa. (Otherwise its −1−1).

0

Hi folks,

I tried solving E, but got WA on test 2(pretest 53) — solution

I tried finding out what's wrong, but no luck!

My solution:

If I just keep adding 'x' to the end, then it won't contribute to a new palindrome substring.

x: A number chosen such that it differs from the last two distinct numbers. I just keep track of the last two distinct numbers.

Example:

n=4 k=2

array = 2 2 3 3

last two distinct numbers — 2,3 -> so we can place 1 at the end => 2 2 3 3 1

last two distinct numbers — 1,3 -> so we can place 2 at the end => 2 2 3 3 1 2

Any help will be much appreciated!

→ Reply

    »
    Wafarm
    5 months ago, show (+1) #
    »
    »
    Wafarm
    5 months ago, hide # ^ |
     
    0

    For example when n = 4

    array = 1 3 2 3

    You can only add 4 but not 1, which your algorithm can’t determine.

For problem C beautiful XOR, we can do the conversion in two steps max, if the msb of b and a are opposite, we can take the safe route and XOR a with a number which will result in all set bits as b excluding msb, and then XOR with log2(a) to set msb of a to zero, further if the msb bits are set, we can directly take the XOR a^b, it'll always be less than a.

→ Reply

For Problem C, I used the relationships a ^ b = c and a ^ c = b. The solution runs in O(1) time per test case.

https://codeforces.com/contest/2162/submission/344334659

~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~

include<bits/stdc++.h>

using namespace std;

int main() { int t; cin >> t;

while (t--)
{
    int a , b;
    cin >> a >> b;

    int x = a^b;

    if(x == 0) cout << 0 << endl;
    else if(a < b && x > a) cout << -1 << endl;
    else
    {
        if(x<=a)
        {
            cout << 1 << endl;
            cout << x << endl;
        }
        else 
        {
            cout << 2 << endl;
            cout << b << " " << a << endl;
        }

    }
}

return 0;

}

In fact, this was a very good competition, but I'm not very good at it, I don't understand why the output of index 0 in question B was fine (the first group in input 1 had 0 but there was no output), I don't quite understand, hope someone can help me explain!

→ Reply
  • »
    »
    d1amondzz
    5 months ago, show (+4) #
    »
    »
    d1amondzz
    5 months ago, hide # ^ |
     
    0

    The output is: the length of the sequence of indices and the sequence of indices in the next line.

    If the length is 0 the sequence is empty, so we don't remove anything, and it's fine because in that case the string was already palindromic, and the empty sequence is considered non-decreasing as stated in the problem.

I'm getting wrong output / not working properly. Can someone please help me find the issue in my code?

Here’s my code:

#include <bits/stdc++.h>
using namespace std;

void solve() {
    long long n, k;
    cin >> n >> k;
    vector<int> v2(n);
    for (int i = 0; i < n; i++) cin >> v2[i];
    vector<int> v(3);
    map<int, int> mpp;
    int j = 2;
    for (int i = n - 1; i >= 0; i--) {
        if (mpp.find(v2[i]) == mpp.end()) {
            mpp[v2[i]]++;
            v[j--] = v2[i];
        }
        if (j == -1) break;
    }
    if (j >= 0) {
        for (int i = 1; i <= 3; i++) {
            if (mpp.find(i) == mpp.end()) {
                v[j--] = i;
                mpp[i]++;
            }
            if (j == -1) break;
        }
    }
    for (int i = 0; i < k; i++) cout << v[i % 3] << " ";
    cout << '\n';
}

int main() {
    ios::sync_with_stdio(false);
    cin.tie(nullptr);
    int t;
    cin >> t;
    while (t--) solve();
}

Please tell me what’s wrong or how to fix it. Thanks!

vidanovic
5 months ago, show #

vidanovic
5 months ago, hide # |
 
0

C's solution is overcomplicated, probably because you wanted to keep it concise, B is formable from A in 2 operations max if it is possible

if MSB(B) > MSB(A) then obviously B cannot be formed from A -> -1, if MSB(B) == MSB(A) then we can just compare their bits and get the required x -> 1 operation if MSB(B) < MSB(A) then first we compare all the bits that are right of A's MSB, and in the 2nd operation we XOR the MSB of A as well -> 2 operations, and all of these x's are guaranteed to be less than A.

→ Reply

The solution for problem G is amazing! I don't think I not have came up with that even if I had two more hours. For those who have solved the problem, would you mind sharing a little bit about your mindset while encountering this kind of construction problems? Thank you!

→ Reply
  • »
    »
    codexistent
    5 months ago, show (+1) #
    »
    »
    codexistent
    5 months ago, hide # ^ |
    ← Rev. 4 →  
    +4

    Here was my thought process for G:

    • We are asked to find a valid construction... but for what nns is a construction even possible? - So we try a couple of sample cases around n=7−9n=7−9. In all of these cases a construction is possible... hm, perhaps it is always possible from n=3n=3 onwards? Let us try proving this hypothesis.
    • We use induction, setting n=3n=3 as our base case (in which the square is k=9k=9). From here, is there a method of transitioning to n+1n+1 nodes such that we maintain a square kk?

    • After trying some random methods out, we find the iterative 'add-remove-add' method as specified in the official solution. This proves our hypothesis and solves the problem.

    Hope this helps!

    → Reply

      »
      »
      32826140857649
      5 months ago, show #

+3

Randomized solution for G

Spoiler
    while(1)
    {
        ll sm = 0;
        vector < pair < ll, ll > > edges;
        for(ll i = 1; i <= n; i++)
        {
            if(i == 1 || i == 2) continue;
            // 2...i-1
            ll x = rng() % i;
            while(x == 0 || x == 1) x = rng() % i;
            sm += i*x;
            edges.push_back({i, x});
        }
        for(ll i = 2; i <= n; i++)
        {
            if(is_sq(sm + i))
            {
                edges.push_back({1, i});
                for(const auto &i : edges) cout << i.first << ' ' << i.second << endl;
                return;
            }
        }
    }
→ Reply

I think the time complexity for solution F is written wrong by mistake. It is written as O(n+m) per testcase but I think it is O(n * m) per testcase. 

for (int i = 0; i < m; i++){
   cin >> l[i] >> r[i];
        st[l[i]] = 1;
        en[r[i]] = 1;
        for (int j = l[i]; j <= r[i]; j++){
            f[j]++;
        }
    }
}

See the above code it is running for O(m * Interval Size) in the worst case all the interval sizes are n so the effective time complexity becomes O(m * n).

Astroflexx

0

My solution for problem EE:

  • In a string, if the substring [l,r][l,r] is a palindrome, then the substring [l+1,r−1][l+1,r−1] must also be a palindrome.
  • Lets try to solve the problem for k=1k=1. If the suffix [l,n][l,n] is a palindrome, we must not push back al−1al−1, otherwise [l−1,n+1][l−1,n+1] will now become a new palindrome.
  • Thus, keep a set of numbers from 11 to nn, and for every suffix [l,n][l,n] that is a palindrome, erase al−1al−1 from the set. Also, we must not push back anan as well, so erase anan from the set.
  • Finding all suffixes that are palindromes can be done using Manacher's algorithm.
  • Lets take any remaining number in the set to push back. Let this number be xx.
  • Then, the next number that we can push back can be any number in the range [1,n]−an,x[1,n]−an,x (since [...,an,x,an][...,an,x,an] and [...,an,x,x][...,an,x,x] will form palindromes).
  • Let the next number we push back be yy.
  • Now to push back the remaining k−2k−2 numbers, we can choose any k−2k−2 numbers in [1,n]−x,y[1,n]−x,y.
  • Since we did not form any new palindromes, there will not be any new palindromes forming due to these numbers either. Thus we have minimized the number of palindromes.

Submission

→ Reply

I wrote an O(1) solution for G

345123116

just using the observation that we can always make the sum of product equal to n^2 and starting from the tree where all are connected to 1. Let diff be the increase in sum required ie. n^2-(n(n+1)/2-1). Now adjust n to a place such that diff comes between n and 2n-1. now it can simply be achieved by one or two place changed. Ive proved that it will always work for n>5.

zzash
5 months ago, show #

zzash
5 months ago, hide # |
 
0 
#include <bits/stdc++.h>
#define ll long long
using namespace std;
void read_fast(){
    ios::sync_with_stdio(0);
    cin.tie(0);
    cout.tie(0);
#ifndef ONLINE_JUDGE
    freopen("1.in","r",stdin);
#endif
}

int op(int l,int r){
    int sum=0;
    cout<<1<<' '<<l<<" "<<r <<endl;
    fflush(stdout);
    cin>>sum;

    return sum;
}

int fp(int l,int r){
    int sum=0;
    cout<<2<<' '<<l<<" "<<r<<endl;
    fflush(stdout);
    cin>>sum;

    return sum;
}

void ans(int l,int r){
    cout<<"!"<<' '<<l<<" "<<r<<endl;
    fflush(stdout);
    return;
}
bool check_same(int a,int b){
    return a==b;
}
bool check(int l,int r,int op_s,int fp_s){
    return op_s+(r-l+1)==fp_s;
}


void solve(){
    int len;
    cin>>len;
    int l=1,r=len;
    int spp_r=0,spp_l=0,mid=0,ans_l=0,ans_r=0;
    if (check(l,r,op(l,r),fp(l,r)))ans(l,r);
    int first=1;
    while (1){
        mid=(r-l)/2;
        mid+=1;
        if (first)
        {int temp_a=op(mid,r),temp_b=fp(mid,r),temp_c=op(l,mid),temp_d=fp(l,mid);
        if (!check(l,mid,temp_c,temp_d)&& !check(mid,r,temp_a,temp_b)&& !check_same(temp_a,temp_b)&& !check_same(temp_c,temp_d)){
            spp_r=r;
            spp_l=l;
            first=0;
            r=mid;
            continue;
        }}
        int temp_x=op(l,mid),temp_y=fp(l,mid);
        if (check(l,mid,temp_x,temp_y)){ans_l=l;if (spp_l!=0&&spp_r!=0){l=spp_l;r=spp_r;}} else r=mid;
        if (check_same(temp_x,temp_y))l=mid;
        if (check(mid,r,op(mid,r),fp(mid,r))){ans_r=r;if(ans_l)ans(ans_l,ans_r);return;}

    }



}


int main() {
    int t;
    cin>>t;
    while (t--)solve();
    return 0;
}

why i always get Idleness limit exceeded.I have used ffulsh for my cout.

wuhudsm Is your implementation correct for this problem? Because it doesn’t print any binary string. Also, I think it’s a bit hard to understand why the second state is better. If I understand correctly, you’re saying:

In the first state, we get

  • r[2]−l[0]+1−(r[0]−l[1]+1)−(r[1]−l[2]+1)r[2]−l[0]+1−(r[0]−l[1]+1)−(r[1]−l[2]+1) indices of type 1,

  • 00 indices of type 2, and

  • (r[0]−l[1]+1)+(r[1]−l[2]+1)(r[0]−l[1]+1)+(r[1]−l[2]+1) indices of type 3.

In the second state, we get

  • r[2]−l[0]+1r[2]−l[0]+1 indices of type 1, and

  • 00 of type 2 and type 3.

Therefore, we can say that it is always optimal to have fewer type 3 indices when we use the same number of indices for type 1 or type 2.

I have a weird solution for problem G: 347103995

I find the minimum x such that S=1⋅2+2⋅3+S=1⋅2+2⋅3+ ... +(n−1)⋅n≤x2+(n−1)⋅n≤x2. Then I greedily increase SS until S=xS=x. And somehow it worked xD
